stem:
The axolotl, a salamander native to a few lakes near Mexico City, retains juvenile features such as external gills throughout its entire life. Unlike most amphibians, it does not undergo a full metamorphosis into a land-dwelling adult form. Because of this, axolotls remain in the water even after reaching sexual maturity.

Based on the text, which choice most logically completes the text?

One can reasonably conclude that axolotls ______

choices.text: are capable of surviving on land for long periods of time.
are more closely related to fish than to other salamanders.
lose their gills once they reach sexual maturity.
reproduce without ever leaving an aquatic environment.

explanation: The passage says axolotls keep their gills and never leave the water, yet still reach sexual maturity — so reproduction must occur in water.

stem:
A small bakery in a college town reported that its sales of coffee and pastries spiked sharply during the two weeks before final exams each semester. The owner noticed that most of the extra customers arrived between 9 p.m. and midnight, a period when the shop had previously been nearly empty. In response, she extended the shop's hours during finals week to accommodate the new demand.

Which choice most logically completes the text?

choices.text: The bakery's daytime sales declined sharply during finals week.
Many students in the town appear to study late at night during finals.
College students prefer pastries to other late-night foods.
The bakery will permanently stay open until midnight year-round.

explanation: The sales spike concentrated in the 9 p.m.–midnight window during finals directly supports that students are studying — and buying snacks — late at night.

stem:
The coast redwood (Sequoia sempervirens) grows almost exclusively in a narrow strip of foggy coastline in northern California and southern Oregon. Scientists have found that the trees absorb a significant portion of their water directly from fog that drifts in from the ocean each morning. In drier inland areas only a short distance away, coast redwoods do not grow at all.

Which statement is most strongly supported by the text?

choices.text: Coast redwoods cannot absorb water through their roots.
Rainfall plays no role in the life cycle of coast redwoods.
Fog is essential to the survival of coast redwoods in their natural range.
Coast redwoods would grow faster if the coastal fog were eliminated.

explanation: The trees grow only where there is fog, rely on fog for water, and do not grow in nearby drier areas — so fog must be essential to their survival.

stem:
Honeybees communicate the location of a food source to their hive mates through a series of movements known as the waggle dance. The angle of the dance relative to vertical indicates the direction of the food relative to the sun, and the duration of the waggling segment indicates the distance to the food. Bees that observe the dance can then fly directly to the food source without any further guidance.

Which choice most logically completes the text?

choices.text: The waggle dance evolved more recently than other bee behaviors.
Honeybees use the waggle dance primarily to warn other bees of danger.
Honeybees are able to convey specific spatial information to one another without vocal sounds.
Most bees ignore the waggle dance and search for food at random.

explanation: Direction and distance are both spatial information, and the bees communicate this through movement alone — no vocal sounds mentioned or required.

stem:
The Antikythera mechanism, a bronze device recovered from an ancient Greek shipwreck, is widely regarded as the earliest known analog computer. Built more than two thousand years ago, it used a complex system of interlocking gears to predict astronomical positions and eclipses decades in advance. When it was first discovered in 1901, no one believed that such a sophisticated machine could have existed at the time it was made.

Which choice most logically completes the text?

choices.text: The Antikythera mechanism was the only scientific instrument ever built by the Greeks.
Ancient Greek astronomers rarely used mathematical tools.
The discovery of the Antikythera mechanism changed assumptions about the technological capabilities of ancient Greece.
Modern computers were directly modeled on the Antikythera mechanism.

explanation: Researchers initially thought such sophistication was impossible for that era, so the mechanism's existence must have forced them to revise what ancient Greeks were capable of building.

stem:
A marine biologist studying bottlenose dolphins in Shark Bay, Australia, observed that some dolphins place marine sponges over their snouts while foraging along the sandy seafloor. Only certain dolphins engage in this behavior, and calves appear to learn it primarily from their mothers rather than from any other members of the pod. No similar behavior has been observed in bottlenose dolphins in other regions.

Which choice most logically completes the text?

choices.text: All bottlenose dolphins instinctively know how to use tools from birth.
Bottlenose dolphins in Shark Bay are genetically distinct from those elsewhere.
Sponges are the most common object on the Shark Bay seafloor.
Sponge-carrying is a learned cultural behavior passed from mother to offspring.

explanation: Since calves learn it from their mothers and it's limited to one population, the behavior fits the definition of a learned, culturally transmitted trait.

stem:
In an experiment on plant growth, two groups of identical bean seedlings were placed in rooms that were kept at the same temperature and given the same amount of water. The first group was exposed to twelve hours of sunlight per day, while the second group was kept in near-total darkness. After three weeks, the seedlings in the first group were significantly taller and greener than those in the second group.

Which choice most logically completes the text?

choices.text: The amount of sunlight a bean plant receives affects its growth and appearance.
Water is not necessary for bean plants to grow.
Temperature is the single most important factor in plant growth.
Bean plants can grow equally well in light and darkness.

explanation: Temperature and water were held constant, so the difference in height and color must be due to the only variable that changed — sunlight.

stem:
In his 2018 study of reading habits among middle school students, education researcher Marcus Whitfield found that students who had access to a personal bookshelf at home containing at least twenty books read, on average, significantly more pages of independent reading each week than students without such a shelf. This held true even when the students lived in households with comparable income levels and attended the same schools. Whitfield suggested that the mere presence of accessible books, independent of family wealth, plays a meaningful role in shaping a student's relationship with reading.

Which choice most logically completes the text?

choices.text: Household income is the primary factor that determines how much middle school students read for pleasure.
Middle school students who read more pages per week tend to come from wealthier families than those who read less.
Physical access to books at home may influence students' reading behavior beyond what socioeconomic factors alone can explain.
Schools that encourage home libraries produce students with higher standardized test scores.

explanation: Whitfield held income and school constant and still found a reading difference tied to bookshelf access, which points to access itself — beyond socioeconomic factors — as influential.

stem:
Archaeologists excavating a site in southern Peru uncovered a set of woven textiles dyed a deep, uniform shade of purple. Chemical analysis revealed that the dye came from a species of sea snail found only along the Pacific coast, more than three hundred kilometers from the excavation site. The textiles themselves were made from the wool of highland camelids, which are not native to the coast.

Which choice most logically completes the text?

choices.text: Sea snails were once common throughout the Peruvian highlands.
Highland camelids were the only source of wool used throughout ancient Peru.
The textiles were produced by traveling artisans who did not live in either region.
The people at the site likely participated in trade networks that connected coastal and highland regions.

explanation: The textiles combine materials unique to the coast and the highlands, which could only come together through trade or exchange between the two regions.

stem:
Researchers studying the common octopus found that individuals placed in unfamiliar tanks frequently rearranged loose rocks and shells to form enclosed shelters near one wall. When the same octopuses were later moved to identical tanks that already contained pre-built shelters, most of them dismantled the existing shelters and reconstructed their own. The researchers noted that the final shelters tended to be similar in size and shape to the originals, despite being rebuilt from scratch.

Which statement is most strongly supported by the text?

choices.text: Octopuses prefer living in shelters built by other octopuses.
Common octopuses are unable to recognize shelters built by humans.
The common octopus appears to have an individual preference in how its shelter is constructed.
Octopuses build shelters only when placed in small enclosures.

explanation: The octopuses rejected pre-built shelters and rebuilt ones of similar size and shape — a pattern that points to personal preference rather than random behavior.

stem:
Between 1880 and 1910, a wave of apartment buildings with flat, unornamented facades rose in several major European cities, replacing older structures decorated with elaborate carvings and moldings. Contemporary architectural critics often dismissed the new buildings as cold or impersonal, yet housing records from the period show that apartments in these buildings filled quickly and rented for prices comparable to those in nearby, more traditional buildings.

Which choice most logically completes the text?

choices.text: Residents of the period were generally unwilling to live in buildings that lacked decorative ornamentation.
Ornamented buildings were more expensive to construct than buildings with flat facades.
The aesthetic judgments of architectural critics did not always match the preferences reflected in residents' actual housing choices.
The new apartment buildings were structurally safer than the traditional ones they replaced.

explanation: Critics panned the plain buildings as cold, but people rented them just as readily and for the same rates — a gap between critical opinion and real-world preference.

stem:
A team of biologists studying a remote population of cave-dwelling fish noticed that the fish had no functioning eyes, even though their closest surface-dwelling relatives possessed fully developed vision. Fossil evidence from nearby sediment layers showed that ancestral members of the cave population had normal eyes, and genetic analysis indicated that the genes responsible for eye development were still present in the current population, though they were no longer expressed during growth.

Which choice most logically completes the text?

choices.text: The loss of vision in the cave fish resulted from changes in gene expression rather than from the disappearance of the relevant genes themselves.
The cave fish lost their ability to see because the genes for eye development were removed from their DNA.
The cave environment caused the fish to evolve new, more useful senses unrelated to vision.
The ancestors of the cave fish also lived without functioning eyes.

explanation: The eye-development genes are still there but simply aren't being turned on — so the loss of vision has to come from expression, not deletion.

stem:
Urban ecologist Priya Rao analyzed bird populations in a major city over a twenty-year span. She found that species capable of varying the pitch and timing of their songs — such as great tits and house sparrows — maintained stable populations despite steady increases in traffic and construction noise. By contrast, species whose songs were more rigidly fixed, such as certain forest thrushes, declined sharply in the same neighborhoods over the same period.

Which choice most logically completes the text?

choices.text: The ability to adjust vocal signals may give some bird species an advantage in noisy urban environments.
Urban noise has no measurable effect on the survival of any bird species.
Forest thrushes are more sensitive to pollution than other types of songbirds.
Great tits and house sparrows produce louder songs than forest thrushes do.

explanation: Species that can adjust their song held steady while species with fixed songs declined under the same noise pressure, pointing directly to vocal flexibility as the advantage.

stem:
In the early twentieth century, American novelist Willa Cather set several of her most acclaimed works on the Nebraska prairie, drawing closely on the landscapes and immigrant communities she had known as a child. Literary critics of her day sometimes faulted her for writing about what they considered an unsophisticated, regional subject. Cather, however, argued that serious literature could arise from any setting, so long as the writer observed it with care and honesty.

Which choice most logically completes the text?

choices.text: Cather's critics preferred novels set in the American prairie to novels set in European cities.
Cather believed that literary value depended on the quality of observation rather than on the prestige of a subject.
The Nebraska prairie was the only region Cather considered worth writing about.
Cather's novels were widely read only after the settings she described had disappeared.

explanation: Cather defended her choice by claiming any setting can yield serious literature with careful, honest observation — locating value in craft, not subject matter.

stem:
A team of engineers tested a new design of wind turbine blade in a controlled wind tunnel. Under steady wind speeds, the new blade produced only slightly more electricity than a traditional blade of the same length. However, when the wind speed was changed rapidly and unpredictably, the new blade continued to generate power consistently, while the traditional blade's output fluctuated sharply and sometimes dropped to near zero.

Which statement is most strongly supported by the text?

choices.text: The new blade design is superior for all wind conditions.
Traditional wind turbine blades cannot generate electricity in unsteady wind.
The new blade design generates less electricity at high wind speeds than the traditional blade.
The new blade design is particularly well suited to environments with highly variable wind.

explanation: The new blade's biggest advantage emerged under rapidly changing winds, not under steady ones — so its strength is specifically in variable conditions.

stem:
A museum curator reviewing nineteenth-century landscape paintings observed that works depicting rural scenes from a specific valley in France routinely sold for far higher prices than comparable works depicting other regions — but only after a well-known poet publicly praised the valley in a popular memoir. Sales records from before the memoir's publication showed no such price premium for paintings of that valley.

Which choice most logically completes the text?

choices.text: Paintings of French landscapes were generally more valuable than those of other European landscapes.
The quality of landscape paintings depicting the valley improved after the memoir was published.
Nineteenth-century buyers preferred poetry to landscape paintings.
The poet's memoir appears to have influenced the market value of paintings associated with the valley.

explanation: The price premium appeared only after the memoir's release, lining up the shift in market value with the new public attention the valley received from the poet.

stem:
In a 2021 study of decision-making in emergency room physicians, researchers found that doctors who had worked overlapping shifts longer than ten hours were no more likely to misdiagnose unusual conditions than doctors working standard shifts — but only when they had access to a second colleague who reviewed their initial assessments. When fatigued doctors worked alone, their rate of misdiagnosis for unusual conditions rose sharply, even though their accuracy on routine cases remained comparable to that of rested doctors.

Which choice most logically completes the text?

choices.text: Peer review during extended shifts appears to mitigate the effects of fatigue specifically on the diagnosis of unusual conditions.
Extended shifts in emergency medicine impair physicians' diagnostic abilities across all types of cases, regardless of whether colleagues are available.
Routine cases are inherently easier to diagnose correctly than unusual ones, even for well-rested physicians.
Emergency room physicians should never work shifts longer than ten hours under any circumstances.

explanation: Fatigue only hurt accuracy on unusual cases and only when doctors worked alone; a reviewing colleague erased that gap, so peer review specifically offsets fatigue on hard diagnoses.

stem:
Historian Elena Márquez, writing about seventeenth-century Amsterdam, notes that city officials required all newly constructed canal houses to conform to strict width limits, even as property owners competed to build the most impressive facades. Paradoxically, this constraint appears to have encouraged rather than suppressed architectural innovation: because builders could not make their houses wider, they invested heavily in ornamenting the narrow fronts with distinctive gables, carved stonework, and elaborate window arrangements. Márquez argues that surviving canal houses from this period are most easily distinguished from one another not by their overall dimensions but by these decorative details.

Which choice most logically completes the text?

choices.text: Strict width limits on canal houses ensured that all seventeenth-century Amsterdam homes ended up looking essentially identical to one another.
Restrictions on one dimension of a design can redirect creative effort toward other features rather than eliminating creative effort altogether.
Amsterdam's regulations on canal house widths were eventually abandoned because they prevented any form of architectural creativity.
Decorative features such as gables and carved stonework were more common in Amsterdam than in any other European city of the period.

explanation: Width limits pushed builders to express individuality through facade ornamentation, which illustrates how a restriction on one dimension can redirect — rather than kill — creative effort.

stem:
A recent long-term study of a tropical rainforest tracked populations of fig trees and the species of wasps that pollinate them. Researchers found that although each fig species relied on only a single, specialized species of wasp for pollination, most wasp species could survive short periods without a host fig by feeding on alternative nectar sources. During a multi-year drought, several fig tree species experienced prolonged failures to fruit, yet the wasps associated with them persisted, and pollination resumed rapidly once fruiting returned to normal.

Which statement is most strongly supported by the text?

choices.text: Fig trees would be able to reproduce successfully in the absence of their pollinating wasps.
The ability of the wasps to exploit alternative food sources helps stabilize an otherwise fragile mutualistic relationship during periods of environmental stress.
The wasps in this rainforest depend on fig trees more than the fig trees depend on the wasps.
Drought conditions in tropical rainforests typically eliminate most pollinator species within a few years.

explanation: The wasps' access to backup nectar kept them alive through fig failures, so pollination could resume — a clear case of alternative food sources buffering a one-to-one mutualism under stress.

stem:
In a study of collaborative problem-solving, psychologist Hana Okoro assembled groups of four participants and asked each group to work through a series of logic puzzles. Half of the groups were told that one specific member had been identified as the team's "designated leader" and should coordinate discussion; the other half received no such designation. Okoro found that the groups without designated leaders solved more puzzles overall, but that their members also reported higher levels of frustration during the task. In groups with a designated leader, puzzle performance was slightly lower, yet members reported feeling more comfortable and more willing to speak up.

Which choice most logically completes the text?

choices.text: Formally designating a leader in a small problem-solving group appears to trade a modest reduction in performance for a more comfortable group experience.
Groups without designated leaders are unable to produce members who feel comfortable speaking during collaborative tasks.
Members of groups with designated leaders solve logic puzzles more accurately than members of any other kind of group.
Frustration during problem-solving is a reliable sign that a group is about to fail the task it has been assigned.

explanation: Leaderless groups solved more puzzles but felt worse; led groups felt better but solved slightly fewer — a direct trade between raw performance and group comfort.
